key_finding: deltaHED, a metric quantifying evolutionary divergence between germline and tumor-acquired HLA class I alleles, was associated with higher TMB and neoantigen load yet predicted worse PFS and OS under PD-1 blockade across three cancer types (RM/NPC, melanoma, ESCC). In ESCC the association was specific to the immunotherapy arm, and high deltaHED correlated with more mutations in antigen-processing and TCR pathways.
intervention: PD-1 blockade (monotherapy in RM/NPC and melanoma; PD-1 + chemotherapy in ESCC)
outcome: High deltaHED predicted worse PFS and OS on PD-1 blockade despite higher TMB/neoantigen load
effect_size: 3 cohorts: n=164 (RM/NPC POLARIS-02), n=88 (melanoma), n=477 (ESCC JUPITER-06); survival difference specific to the immunotherapy arm of ESCC

confidence.reasoning: Three independent cohorts including two randomized trials, but findings are retrospective biomarker analyses without functional validation; effect direction is contrary to prior HED literature and needs prospective confirmation.

caveats: Retrospective biomarker analysis
deltaHED combines germline and somatic HLA divergence; attribution between components is not fully resolved
Mechanistic link to antigen-processing mutations is correlative
ESCC cohort received combination with chemotherapy, complicating interpretation
surprising_why: Contradicts the prevailing TMB/neoantigen-centric view: although high deltaHED tracked with higher TMB and neoantigen burden, it predicted worse rather than better PD-1 outcomes, suggesting HLA evolutionary divergence can mark immune escape rather than broad presentation capacity.

key_finding: Single-cell spatial transcriptomics of 27 cSCC patients across three cohorts identified six distinct tissue niches that stratify response to neoadjuvant PD-1/PD-L1 blockade: high antigen presentation, B/plasma cell, and inflammatory keratinocyte niches were enriched in responders, while proliferative keratinocyte, low-antigen-presentation myeloid, and fibroblast-rich EMT niches dominated non-responders. Pretreatment niche profiling outperformed PD-L1 status in predicting pathologic response.
intervention: Neoadjuvant PD-1/PD-L1 blockade in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma
outcome: Six spatial niches differentially enriched in responders vs non-responders; niche profiling outperformed PD-L1 IHC
effect_size: 27 patients across three cohorts (including two phase II trials); qualitative advantage of niche-based prediction over PD-L1 status

confidence.reasoning: Includes two phase II trials and multi-timepoint sampling, but total sample size is modest (27 patients) and validation is internal across the three cohorts; findings are associative.

caveats: Small cohort (n=27)
Tissue microarray cores (1.7 mm) may not capture full tumor heterogeneity
Observational associations; no causal perturbation of niches
cSCC-specific — generalization untested

key_finding: Transient upregulation of PD-1 on clonally expanding human CD4+ and CD8+ T cells protects them from premature restimulation-induced cell death (RICD) by attenuating proximal TCR and CD28 signaling and modulating pro/anti-apoptotic protein expression. PD-L1 engagement rescued effector T cells from RICD in a dose- and synapse-proximity-dependent manner, with stronger rescue under CD28 co-ligation.
intervention: PD-L1-coated bead restimulation of expanding human primary T cells; biochemical and proteomic profiling of TCR/CD28 signaling
outcome: PD-1 signaling conferred apoptosis resistance to restimulated effector T cells, particularly PD-1^hi clonally expanding populations
effect_size: Qualitative; dose-dependent PD-L1 rescue of RICD in human primary T cells; no clinical or in vivo quantification

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistic human primary T cell study with biochemical/proteomic support, but confined to in vitro systems without in vivo or patient validation.

caveats: In vitro human T cell system only
No in vivo tumor or patient data
Mechanistic implications for PD-1 blockade-induced T cell loss are inferred
surprising_why: Inverts the traditional framing of PD-1 as a purely death/exhaustion-inducing receptor: homeostatic PD-1 expression protects newly expanding effector T cells from apoptosis, implying anti-PD-1 therapy could paradoxically sensitize certain T cell populations to RICD and alter adaptive immune durability in ways not captured by the simple checkpoint-release model.

key_finding: T cell-intrinsic VISTA enforces CD8+ T cell dysfunction: its deletion enhanced early CD8+ priming and expansion but did not yield durable tumor control because trans-VISTA on myeloid cells and compensatory CTLA-4 upregulation sustained dysfunction. Combining T cell-intrinsic VISTA loss with CTLA-4 blockade improved T cell survival, broadened TCR diversity, and produced stronger tumor regression than CTLA-4 blockade alone; a VISTA-deficient cytotoxic T cell signature correlated with favorable ICI outcomes in patients.
intervention: T cell-specific VISTA deletion in tumor-bearing mice, with and without CTLA-4 blockade
outcome: Enhanced early CD8+ priming/expansion with VISTA loss; synergy with anti-CTLA-4 producing robust tumor regression and broader TCR repertoire
effect_size: Qualitative improvement over anti-CTLA-4 monotherapy; patient correlation with existing ICI outcomes reported

confidence.reasoning: Integrates scRNA-seq, TCR repertoire, and flow cytometry in mice plus a patient correlation, but durability advantage requires combination with CTLA-4 blockade.

caveats: Preclinical mouse models; no clinical trial
Patient correlation is retrospective signature analysis
Direct relevance to anti-PD-1 resistance inferred rather than directly tested

key_finding: In dMMR/MSI-H metastatic colorectal cancer, an 8-gene (4 k-TSP pair) classifier combined with mucinous histology identifies a subgroup (Cluster A + mucinous; 15.3% of patients) that derives substantially greater benefit from anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4 combination versus anti-PD-1-based monotherapy. This composite biomarker improved model fit for a treatment interaction across 163 pooled patients from two independent cohorts.
intervention: Anti-PD-1 ± anti-LAG-3 (Mono) vs anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4 (Combo) in dMMR/MSI-H mCRC
outcome: Cluster A + mucinous subgroup showed a large PFS benefit from Combo over Mono
effect_size: Pooled n=163; k-TSP balanced accuracy 68.1%; adjusted 24-month PFS 72.2% (Combo) vs 13.8% (Mono) in biomarker-positive subgroup; HR 0.10 (95% CI 0.02–0.39; p<0.001)

confidence.reasoning: Training plus independent validation cohort with pre-specified composite biomarker and sizeable HR, but the benefiting subgroup is small (25 patients) and analyses are retrospective across non-randomized comparisons.

caveats: Retrospective analysis; not a randomized comparison
Biomarker-positive subgroup is small (n=25)
Excluded patients on anti-PD-L1 and ECOG ≥2
Prospective validation pending

key_finding: HILPDA sustains KLF5-driven fatty acid synthesis, generating palmitate that augments PD-L1 palmitoylation at cysteine 272 to stabilize membrane PD-L1 and drive immune evasion; TRIM21 catalyzes K63-linked polyubiquitination and degradation of HILPDA, and pharmacologic TRIM21 engagement with fenretinide restores anti-PD-1 efficacy in breast cancer models.
intervention: Fenretinide (TRIM21 engager) to degrade HILPDA, combined with anti-PD-1
outcome: Reduced PD-L1 palmitoylation, increased cytotoxic immunity, improved anti-PD-1 efficacy in breast cancer models
effect_size: Qualitative: restored antitumor responses in mouse breast cancer models

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistically detailed mouse and cell-line study with defined molecular axis (HILPDA-HSP90-KLF5-palmitate-PD-L1 C272); lacks clinical validation and relies on a single tumor type.

caveats: Breast cancer models only
Fenretinide specificity for TRIM21 engagement not fully characterized
No clinical validation
surprising_why: Identifies a novel post-translational modification axis in which lipid-droplet regulator HILPDA drives PD-L1 palmitoylation via KLF5-sustained fatty acid synthesis, linking tumor lipogenesis directly to ICB resistance through a PTM rather than transcriptional PD-L1 regulation.

key_finding: NOTCH3 transcriptionally upregulates the immune checkpoint PVR via RBPJ binding in colorectal cancer, suppressing CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity through enhanced PVR-TIGIT interactions; NOTCH3 depletion synergizes with anti-PD-L1 in mice, and NOTCH3 mutation or low expression independently predicts improved survival in ICB-treated CRC and pan-cancer cohorts.
intervention: Anti-NOTCH3 (genetic depletion) combined with anti-PD-L1
outcome: Inhibited tumor growth and increased CD8+ T cell infiltration in mouse xenografts; improved survival in ICB-treated patients with NOTCH3 mutation/low expression
effect_size: Qualitative; 102-patient institutional cohort plus MSKCC validation

confidence.reasoning: Multi-modal study with TCGA multi-omics, scRNA-seq, in vitro mechanistic assays, mouse model, and two clinical cohorts; anti-NOTCH3 intervention is genetic, not yet pharmacologic.

caveats: No pharmacologic NOTCH3 inhibitor tested in vivo
Clinical correlations are retrospective

key_finding: PKMYT1 is overexpressed in castration-resistant prostate cancer and suppresses antitumor immunity; its inhibition with RP-6306 activates the cGAS-STING pathway, potentiates type I/II IFN signaling, upregulates CCL5 and CXCL10, and enhances CD8+ T cell infiltration and anti-PD-L1 efficacy in syngeneic mouse models.
intervention: PKMYT1 inhibitor RP-6306 alone or combined with anti-PD-L1
outcome: Activated cGAS-STING, increased activated CD8+ T cell infiltration, significant tumor suppression in vivo
effect_size: Qualitative: significant tumor suppression in syngeneic mouse CRPC models

confidence.reasoning: Combines bioinformatic analyses, IHC in a clinical PCa cohort, mechanistic cell/mouse studies, and scRNA-seq; no prospective clinical data.

caveats: Preclinical efficacy only
cGAS-STING activation mechanism downstream of PKMYT1 inhibition not fully dissected
Generalization beyond CRPC not addressed

key_finding: Lactate drives AARS1-catalyzed lysine lactylation of PD-L1 at K280 in its intracellular domain, which blocks HUWE1-mediated ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, thereby stabilizing PD-L1 and impairing CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity; sodium lactate enhances anti-PD-L1 efficacy in preclinical models and K280 lactylation correlates with advanced NSCLC stage and poor survival.
intervention: Sodium lactate (NaLa) administration combined with anti-PD-L1; genetic perturbation of AARS1
outcome: Enhanced efficacy of anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy; clinical correlation with advanced NSCLC stage and poor survival
effect_size: Qualitative; clinical association with NSCLC stage and survival

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistically detailed with identification of lactyltransferase (AARS1), specific residue (K280), and competing E3 ligase (HUWE1); supported by in vivo mouse data and NSCLC clinical correlations.

caveats: NSCLC clinical cohort size not specified
Paradoxical finding that exogenous lactate enhances anti-PD-L1 efficacy requires careful mechanistic reconciliation
No clinical trial of lactate plus ICB
surprising_why: Reveals that PD-L1 is stabilized by lysine lactylation at K280 which blocks HUWE1-mediated ubiquitination, a previously unappreciated metabolite-driven PTM linking tumor microenvironment lactate to ICB resistance, and counterintuitively implies that exogenous lactate can enhance anti-PD-L1 efficacy.

key_finding: IFN-γ activates IRF1, which transcriptionally upregulates AGPAT3, driving lipid remodeling and accumulation of polyunsaturated ether phospholipids that sensitize tumor cells to ferroptosis; loss of AGPAT3 impairs IFN-γ-mediated tumor elimination, and higher tumor AGPAT3 expression is associated with improved survival in ICI-treated patients.
intervention: Genetic perturbation of AGPAT3
outcome: AGPAT3 loss impaired IFN-γ-mediated tumor killing in vitro and in vivo; higher AGPAT3 associated with immune activation and improved OS under ICI
effect_size: Qualitative: improved OS in ICI-treated patients with higher AGPAT3

confidence.reasoning: Integrates machine learning on ferroptosis gene signatures, scRNA-seq, lipidomics, ChIP-seq/CUT&Tag, and in vitro/in vivo validation.

caveats: ICI-treated cohort details not specified
Causal role of ether phospholipids in ferroptosis sensitization inferred from lipidomics
No pharmacologic AGPAT3 modulator tested

key_finding: In NSCLC, hMENA overexpression defines a TGF-β-driven myofibroblast-like CAF subset in which hMENA and TGF-β signaling reciprocally reinforce each other, driving PD-L1 upregulation, EMT, ECM remodeling, and secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines that expand Tregs and impair CD8+/CD4+ T-cell function. A 9-gene signature derived from this CAF subtype correlates with poor prognosis in TCGA NSCLC and with ICT resistance in SU2C and the OAK phase III trial.
intervention: hMENA silencing in CAFs; derivation of a 9-gene ICT-response signature
outcome: hMENA-high CAFs sustain TGF-β signaling and EMT, upregulate PD-L1, expand Tregs, and associate with poor prognosis and anti-PD-L1/anti-PD-1 resistance
effect_size: Qualitative; signature correlates with ICT resistance in SU2C and OAK (NCT02008227) cohorts

confidence.reasoning: Integrates single-cell atlas, ex vivo CAF experiments, and validation in multiple human cohorts including a phase III trial, but mechanistic work is largely correlative/observational.

caveats: Signature validation is retrospective
Mechanistic causality in patients inferred from in vitro CAF experiments
Abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes

key_finding: In triple-negative breast cancer, TROP2 associates with claudin 7 to regulate tight junctions and create a barrier that excludes T cells; TROP2 loss deregulates tight junction proteins and enables CD8+ T-cell infiltration, while TROP2-directed therapy (hRS7) enhances anti-PD-1 efficacy in a humanized TROP2 syngeneic model and TROP2 expression correlates with lack of response to anti-PD-1 in human breast cancer.
intervention: TROP2 loss-of-function and reconstitution; hRS7 (sacituzumab govitecan antibody component) combined with anti-PD-1
outcome: TROP2 loss or targeting improves T-cell infiltration and effector function and enhances anti-PD-1 response; high TROP2 predicts anti-PD-1 non-response
effect_size: Qualitative: TROP2 inversely correlated with T-cell infiltration

confidence.reasoning: Combines spatial transcriptomics, functional perturbation, syngeneic humanized mouse model, and human ICB data.

caveats: Human ICB correlation cohort size not specified
Preclinical combination efficacy in a single humanized TROP2 syngeneic TNBC model
surprising_why: Reframes TROP2, known primarily as an ADC target, as a functional driver of barrier-mediated immune exclusion via tight-junction regulation, providing a mechanistic rationale beyond cytotoxic payload delivery for combining TROP2 ADCs with anti-PD-1.

key_finding: In microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer, M2-like tumor-associated macrophages express elevated COX1/2 and produce PGE2 that drives TIGIT upregulation on PD-1+ CD8+ T cells, generating a terminally exhausted phenotype that blunts anti-PD-L1 efficacy. M2-TAM depletion, COX2/PGE2-receptor inhibition, or TIGIT blockade reduces TIGIT, restores CD8+ T-cell function, and improves PD-L1 blockade activity; human CRC transcriptomic data corroborate the TAM-COX-TIGIT axis.
intervention: Anti-PD-L1; M2-like TAM depletion; COX2 inhibition; PGE2 receptor inhibition; TIGIT blockade (combinations)
outcome: Reduced TIGIT expression on CD8+ T cells, restored cytotoxicity, improved anti-PD-L1 efficacy in MSS models
effect_size: Qualitative improvement in CD8+ function and anti-PD-L1 efficacy

confidence.reasoning: Mechanism established in mouse models with multiple orthogonal perturbations and supported by single-cell, spatial, and bulk human CRC transcriptomics, though clinical validation is correlative.

caveats: Preclinical models; no clinical trial data
Human data are correlative transcriptomic associations

key_finding: Low-protein diet-induced hypoalbuminemia in C57BL/6 LLC tumor-bearing mice drives resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors by impairing macrophage arginine biosynthesis, producing an immunosuppressive TME with reduced CD8+ T-cell infiltration and expanded TAMs. TAM depletion or dietary arginine supplementation reverses the immunosuppressive phenotype and restores ICI efficacy.
intervention: Low-protein diet; anti-PD-1/PD-L1 ICI; TAM depletion; dietary arginine supplementation
outcome: Hypoalbuminemia reduced ICI response; TAM depletion or arginine supplementation restored CD8+ infiltration and ICI efficacy
effect_size: Qualitative

confidence.reasoning: Mechanistic mouse study with multiple complementary perturbations, but limited to a single syngeneic LLC model without clinical validation.

caveats: Single mouse tumor model (LLC)
Dietary manipulation may not fully recapitulate cancer cachexia biology
No patient-level intervention data
surprising_why: Establishes hypoalbuminemia not merely as a prognostic biomarker but as a direct causal driver of ICI resistance via a macrophage arginine-metabolism circuit, suggesting a nutritional/metabolic intervention (arginine repletion) could restore immunotherapy response.

key_finding: In the phase 3 ATOMIC trial (NCT02912559), adding atezolizumab to adjuvant mFOLFOX6 chemotherapy significantly improved 3-year disease-free survival in resected stage III mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) colon cancer compared with mFOLFOX6 alone.
intervention: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) plus mFOLFOX6 for 6 months, then atezolizumab monotherapy to 12 months total
outcome: Improved 3-year disease-free survival versus chemotherapy alone, at the cost of more grade 3/4 adverse events

confidence.reasoning: Randomized phase 3 trial, 712 patients, prespecified primary endpoint, 40.9-month median follow-up, strong effect size with tight CI.

caveats: Overall survival data not yet mature
Increased grade 3/4 toxicity
Only stage III dMMR subset; does not generalize to MMR-proficient disease

key_finding: In ICI-refractory stage IV melanoma patients, intratumoral LOAd703 (a TMZ-CD40L/4-1BBL-expressing oncolytic adenovirus) plus atezolizumab reprogrammed the myeloid compartment and T-cell landscape, producing local and systemic immune signatures previously associated with ICI response.
intervention: Intratumoral LOAd703 (1e11 or 5e11 viral particles) plus intravenous atezolizumab (1200 mg) every 3 weeks
outcome: Increased TME DC-associated biomarkers, more circulating CD40+ DC-like cells, elevated T-cell infiltration signatures, rise in EM-phenotype CD8+ T cells, and reduced circulating CD4+ T cells including Tregs
effect_size: Qualitative biomarker shifts; n=24 patients in single-arm phase I/II

confidence.reasoning: Single-arm phase I/II, small sample, biomarker-focused endpoints rather than clinical response.

caveats: Single-arm design without randomized comparator
Primary readouts are correlative immune biomarkers, not objective response or survival
Heterogeneous prior-therapy exposure
surprising_why: Demonstrates that myeloid reprogramming via a CD40L/4-1BBL-armed oncolytic vector can restore an ICI-responsive immune signature in melanoma patients with confirmed anti-PD-1 resistance — a population where few interventions reliably re-sensitize to checkpoint blockade.

key_finding: In a phase Ib trial (NCT05400876), dual checkpoint blockade with anti-TIM-3 antibody TQB2618 plus anti-PD-1 penpulimab produced clinically meaningful responses in relapsed/refractory classical Hodgkin lymphoma patients who had already failed prior PD-1/PD-L1 therapy.
intervention: TQB2618 (anti-TIM-3, 600 mg Q3W RP2D) plus penpulimab (anti-PD-1, 200 mg Q3W)
outcome: Objective responses in a post-PD-1-failure cHL population with manageable toxicity
effect_size: ORR 52% (1 CR, 10 PRs) among 21 r/r cHL patients; TRAEs in 86%, grade ≥3 TRAEs in 24%; median DOR and OS not reached at 14.1-month median follow-up

confidence.reasoning: Multicenter phase Ib, small sample (n=21 efficacy-evaluable cHL), single-arm, early follow-up.

caveats: Small single-arm phase Ib cohort
No randomized comparator
DOR and OS not yet reached
Conducted at Chinese sites only
surprising_why: Achieves a 52% ORR by adding TIM-3 blockade to anti-PD-1 in cHL patients who had already progressed on prior PD-1/PD-L1 therapy — a setting where re-engaging checkpoint biology was not expected to yield substantial salvage responses.

key_finding: KLRG1 is upregulated on CD8+ T cells after checkpoint therapy and high KLRG1 expression correlates with anti-PD-1 resistance in melanoma; KLRG1 or N-cadherin (ligand) loss slows tumor growth through CD8+ T, NK, and gamma-delta T cells, and a novel anti-human KLRG1 mAb reduces tumor progression in humanized KLRG1 knock-in mice.
intervention: KLRG1 genetic knockout, tumor N-cadherin deletion, and a novel anti-human KLRG1 monoclonal antibody in murine and humanized-mouse models
outcome: Reduced tumor growth and enhanced human T-cell activation; anti-PD-1-resistant melanoma patient tumors show high KLRG1 on TILs
effect_size: Qualitative/preclinical; correlative patient data

confidence.reasoning: Preclinical mouse study with converging genetic, ligand, and antibody evidence plus patient correlative data; no clinical efficacy yet.

caveats: Predominantly murine and humanized-mouse data
Patient component is correlative, not interventional
No in-human efficacy or safety data
surprising_why: Identifies KLRG1 as a tractable, previously underappreciated inhibitory receptor acting across CD8+ T, NK, and gamma-delta T compartments whose blockade shows therapeutic activity in anti-PD-1-refractory settings, nominating a novel checkpoint axis (KLRG1-cadherin) beyond the established PD-1/CTLA-4/LAG-3/TIM-3 targets.

key_finding: In 4 melanoma patients with late relapse on pembrolizumab after initial response, paired WES revealed acquired JAK1 or JAK2 LOF mutations (with LOH) in 2 patients and a truncating B2M mutation with loss of surface MHC-I in a third. JAK1/2 loss abolished tumor IFN-γ responsiveness, linking IFN-receptor signaling and antigen presentation to acquired anti-PD-1 resistance.
intervention: pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
outcome: acquired clinical resistance after initial tumor regression
effect_size: 3 of 4 relapse tumors harbored candidate resistance lesions (JAK1, JAK2, or B2M); functional loss of IFN-γ signaling and MHC-I surface expression confirmed in vitro

confidence.reasoning: Paired pre/post biopsies with WES plus functional validation (IFN-γ response assays, MHC-I flow) provide biochemically definitive mechanism; replicated conceptually by Shin 2017 and multiple subsequent cohorts.

caveats: n=4; JAK/B2M lesions explain only a fraction of acquired resistance in larger cohorts
Pre-existing subclonal selection vs. de novo acquisition not formally distinguished
Does not address non-genetic resistance mechanisms that dominate in many relapses

key_finding: Biallelic JAK1/2 LOF mutations, previously implicated in acquired resistance, also underlie primary non-response to anti-PD-1 despite high mutational burden. 1 of 23 melanoma and 1 of 16 MMR-deficient CRC non-responders harbored JAK1/2 LOF; matched cell lines failed to induce PD-L1 and ISGs on IFN-γ exposure.
intervention: anti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab)
outcome: primary resistance / absence of reactive PD-L1 induction
effect_size: ~4% of melanoma non-responders and ~6% of MMR-deficient CRC non-responders carried JAK1/2 LOF; complete loss of IFN-γ-induced gene expression in mutant lines

confidence.reasoning: Clinical observation combined with cell-line functional validation; replicates and extends Zaretsky 2016 into primary resistance; biochemically well-grounded.

caveats: JAK1/2 LOF mutations are rare (<5%) and cannot account for the majority of primary resistance
Patient-level causality inferential from n=2 clinical cases
surprising_why: High TMB does not guarantee response when antigen-presentation/IFN-γ sensing is genetically disrupted — refines the then-dominant view that TMB alone predicts ICI benefit.

key_finding: Using LOHHLA to resolve allele-specific HLA-I copy number, HLA-I LOH occurs in ~40% of NSCLCs (TRACERx 100), is subclonal, focal, enriched at metastatic sites and later in evolution, and associated with high subclonal neoantigen burden and APOBEC mutagenesis — evidence that HLA LOH is a positively selected immune-escape event.
outcome: immune escape via allele-specific HLA-I loss; refined neoantigen prediction
effect_size: HLA LOH in ~40% of NSCLC tumors; parallel/convergent events in multiple regions; association with subclonal neoantigen load

confidence.reasoning: Large multi-region WES cohort (TRACERx), new validated method (LOHHLA), multiple convergent signals (timing, focality, neoantigen association). LOHHLA adopted widely; HLA LOH replicated pan-cancer.

caveats: Did not directly test anti-PD-1 response — association with ICI resistance is inferential
LOHHLA accuracy depends on tumor purity and sequencing depth
NSCLC-specific; generalization required later work
surprising_why: Later pan-cancer ICI cohorts showed HLA LOH is not a uniformly deterministic resistance marker — some HLA-LOH patients still respond, and the prognostic/predictive strength depends on which allele is lost, tumor type, and whether residual heterozygous HLA loci can still present the dominant neoantigen. A clear example of a landmark immune-escape mechanism whose clinical deterministic power has softened.

key_finding: Higher nonsynonymous TMB in NSCLC is associated with improved objective response, durable clinical benefit, and PFS under pembrolizumab across two independent cohorts. Benefit also tracked with a molecular smoking signature, neoantigen load, and DNA-repair pathway mutations; neoantigen-specific CD8 responses paralleled regression in one responder.
intervention: pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
outcome: objective response, durable clinical benefit, PFS
effect_size: Discovery + validation NSCLC cohorts; ROC-derived cutoff ~178 nonsynonymous mutations strongly separates DCB from no-benefit; validated independently

confidence.reasoning: Two independent cohorts with WES + clinical data; immunological correlate (neoantigen-specific T cell response) in one patient. Replicated across many tumor types; foundation of TMB as a pan-tumor biomarker.

caveats: Small cohorts (discovery n=16, validation n=18); TMB cutoff derived post-hoc
Nonsynonymous count not directly comparable to panel-based TMB
TMB explains only part of response variance
surprising_why: Subsequent work (KEYNOTE-158, CheckMate-227 biomarker analyses, pan-cancer meta-analyses) has softened the original implied TMB threshold. FDA's pan-tumor pembro approval based on TMB-high has been controversial as some high-TMB tumor types (e.g., certain gliomas) show limited benefit.

key_finding: WES of 64 melanoma patients on CTLA-4 blockade showed somatic mutational load was associated with clinical benefit but insufficient alone to predict benefit. A specific neoantigen 'signature' enriched in responders was identified in a discovery set (n=25) and validated in independent set (n=39); predicted neoepitopes activated autologous T cells ex vivo.
intervention: ipilimumab or tremelimumab (anti-CTLA-4)
outcome: long-term clinical benefit vs minimal/no benefit
effect_size: Mutational load associated with clinical benefit at P=0.01; shared tetrapeptide neoantigen signature enriched responders (did not replicate in later cohorts)

confidence.reasoning: First clear demonstration that TMB associates with ICI benefit in melanoma, with independent validation and functional neoantigen validation.

caveats: Small discovery set (n=25); tetrapeptide signature difficult to reproduce in independent datasets
Mutational load alone underpowered to separate responders from non-responders
Applies to CTLA-4 blockade
surprising_why: The specific 'shared tetrapeptide neoantigen signature' has largely failed to replicate in subsequent larger ICI cohorts — the TMB association survives but the signature itself is widely considered a small-sample artifact.

key_finding: Phase 2 expansion across 12 tumor types: pembrolizumab produced ORR 53% (21% complete responses) in advanced MMR-deficient cancers with durable PFS/OS regardless of tissue of origin. Expansion of neoantigen-reactive T cell clones in a responder supported massive MMR-d neoantigen load as the driver.
intervention: pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
outcome: objective response, durable PFS/OS across histologies
effect_size: ORR 53% (CR 21%) across 86 patients spanning 12 tumor types; median PFS/OS not reached at reporting

confidence.reasoning: Prospective multi-cohort trial with robust imaging endpoints plus functional T-cell correlate; basis of first tissue-agnostic FDA approval (May 2017). Extensively replicated in KEYNOTE-158, -164, -177.

caveats: Single-arm design
Not all MMR-d tumors respond — endometrial, gastric, some pancreatic MMR-d cancers show lower ORR than CRC
MSI/MMR-d testing quality varies between IHC, PCR, NGS methods
surprising_why: Magnitude of cross-histology efficacy established the first tissue-agnostic biomarker-based FDA approval, upending decades of histology-based drug development.

key_finding: A plasma (ctDNA) TMB assay (bTMB) developed on FoundationACT reproducibly identified NSCLC patients who derived PFS benefit from atezolizumab in retrospective analyses of POPLAR (test) and OAK (validation) trials — demonstrating blood-based TMB as a tissue-free biomarker for anti-PD-L1.
intervention: atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1)
outcome: PFS benefit in bTMB-high subgroup
effect_size: At bTMB ≥16, atezolizumab vs docetaxel PFS HR ~0.65 in OAK (validation); monotonic relationship between bTMB and PFS benefit

confidence.reasoning: Assay development with analytical validation followed by retrospective test/validation in two RCTs. Methodology influenced subsequent prospective trials.

caveats: Retrospective analysis; prospective B-F1RST trial did not confirm PFS benefit using bTMB-16 cutoff in first-line monotherapy
bTMB requires adequate ctDNA shedding; cutoffs are assay-specific
bTMB and tissue TMB correlate but are not interchangeable
surprising_why: Despite strong retrospective signal, prospective B-F1RST and BFAST did not clearly confirm bTMB as a first-line predictive biomarker, and bTMB has not achieved broad regulatory adoption — tempering initial enthusiasm.

key_finding: WES of ccRCC from 35 anti-PD-1-treated patients identified LOF mutations in PBRM1 (PBAF SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling subunit) as enriched in clinical responders (P=0.012), validated in an independent cohort of 63 ccRCC patients (P=0.0071). PBAF-deficient models showed altered JAK-STAT, hypoxia, and immune-signaling programs, suggesting PBRM1 loss rewires tumor-intrinsic signaling to increase ICI sensitivity.
intervention: nivolumab / anti-PD-1 (± anti-CTLA-4 in validation)
outcome: clinical benefit (response/PFS)
effect_size: PBRM1 LOF enriched in clinical benefit group in discovery (P=0.012, n=35) and validation (P=0.0071, n=63) cohorts

confidence.reasoning: Two independent cohorts with consistent directionality and plausible mechanism, but subsequent replication has been mixed (see surprising_why).

caveats: Small discovery cohort with modest p-values
ccRCC is TMB-low; signal may reflect unique renal biology
Direct causal experiments in syngeneic models limited
surprising_why: Subsequent larger analyses (Braun et al. 2020 Nat Med on CheckMate-009/010/025, IMmotion150/151) have failed to cleanly replicate PBRM1 as a predictor of ICI benefit in ccRCC — some cohorts show no association or opposite trends. A classic landmark-biomarker replicability failure.

key_finding: In vivo pooled CRISPR-Cas9 screen in B16 melanoma across 2,368 genes identified Ptpn2 loss in tumor cells as a sensitizer to anti-PD-1, acting by amplifying IFN-γ/JAK-STAT signaling to increase antigen presentation. The screen also re-identified PD-L1, CD47, and IFN-γ pathway components as canonical modulators.
intervention: CRISPR knockout of tumor-expressed genes (notably Ptpn2) + anti-PD-1/GVAX
outcome: Ptpn2-null tumors showed markedly enhanced rejection under checkpoint blockade; IFN-γ pathway KOs conferred resistance
effect_size: Ptpn2 sgRNAs strongly depleted from treated tumors (log2 FC ~-2 to -3); resistance hits (Jak1, Stat1, B2m, Tap1) strongly enriched

confidence.reasoning: Large unbiased in vivo screen with arrayed validation, orthogonal mechanistic follow-up, and concordance with prior human genetics. PTPN2 since validated clinically (ABBV-514-class inhibitors in trials).

caveats: Single tumor line (B16) with GVAX+anti-PD-1
Library covered only 2,368 genes, not genome-wide
Stromal/host modifiers not captured
surprising_why: PTPN2 had not previously been nominated as a tumor-intrinsic immune-evasion phosphatase; identifying a druggable negative regulator of IFN-γ signaling in tumor cells (rather than T cells) reframed PTPN2/PTP1B as oncology targets.

key_finding: Tumor-intrinsic WNT/β-catenin activation causes T-cell exclusion from the melanoma microenvironment and primary resistance to anti-PD-L1/anti-CTLA-4. In TCGA melanoma, β-catenin activation anti-correlated with a CD8 signature; in Braf/Pten/CAT-STA autochthonous mice, β-catenin-active tumors failed to recruit CD103+ Batf3+ DCs (via reduced CCL4), preventing T-cell priming.
intervention: Genetic β-catenin activation in autochthonous murine melanoma; anti-PD-L1 + anti-CTLA-4
outcome: β-catenin-active tumors non-T-cell-inflamed, lacked DC recruitment, refractory to dual checkpoint blockade
effect_size: ~48% of non-T-cell-inflamed human melanomas showed active β-catenin vs minimal activation in inflamed tumors; near-complete loss of intratumoral CD8 and ICI efficacy in β-catenin-active mice

confidence.reasoning: Coupled human genomic correlation (TCGA) with causal autochthonous mouse genetics and mechanistic dissection to CCL4/Batf3-DC recruitment. Extended to other tumor types; validated by multiple labs.

caveats: β-catenin activation is one of several drivers of the 'cold' phenotype — not necessary or sufficient across cohorts
Clinical translation of WNT inhibitors + ICI limited by toxicity
Autochthonous model uses single driver combination (BRAF/PTEN)
surprising_why: Reframed a classical developmental/oncogenic pathway as a direct cause of immune exclusion via impaired DC recruitment, at a time when resistance was largely framed around T-cell-intrinsic exhaustion. Subsequent clinical cohorts have found weaker and more context-dependent correlations between β-catenin activation and ICI non-response than the mouse model predicted.

key_finding: PTEN loss in melanoma activates PI3K-AKT, increases immunosuppressive cytokines (VEGF, CCL2), reduces autophagy-dependent T-cell killing, and decreases intratumoral T-cell infiltration. PTEN loss correlated with poorer TIL expansion and worse anti-PD-1 outcomes in a small cohort; a selective PI3Kβ inhibitor restored sensitivity in mice.
intervention: Genetic PTEN loss in melanoma models; PI3Kβ inhibitor (GSK2636771) + anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4
outcome: PTEN-null tumors had reduced CD8 infiltration and impaired ICI response; PI3Kβ inhibition rescued efficacy in vivo
effect_size: Small melanoma cohort (~39 patients) showed reduced intratumoral CD8 and worse PFS on anti-PD-1; significant tumor-growth inhibition with PI3Kβi+ICI in mice

confidence.reasoning: Mechanism triangulated across human tumor samples, isogenic cell lines, and syngeneic mouse models with pharmacologic rescue. PTEN-loss–ICI resistance association replicated in melanoma and pan-cancer analyses.

caveats: Human cohort small and retrospective
Clinical PI3Kβ + ICI combinations have produced mixed early-phase results with toxicity limiting synergy
Effect of PTEN loss on ICI response is context-specific across histologies

key_finding: WES/RNA-seq of pretreatment melanoma biopsies (n=38) under anti-PD-1 defined the IPRES (Innate anti-PD-1 Resistance) transcriptional signature — coordinated upregulation of EMT, cell adhesion, ECM remodeling, angiogenesis, and wound-healing genes — in innately resistant tumors. Higher TMB and BRCA2 mutations associated with response; MAPK-inhibitor programs overlapped with IPRES, suggesting non-genomic MAPKi cross-resistance.
intervention: Anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab/nivolumab) in metastatic melanoma
outcome: IPRES-high tumors enriched among non-responders; signature recurrent across independent cancer cohorts
effect_size: High TMB associated with improved OS (modest P-value); IPRES separated responders from non-responders in discovery cohort

confidence.reasoning: Discovery cohort modest (n=28 with paired mutanome+transcriptome); IPRES partially supported by downstream TGF-β/EMT/stromal work but has not replicated cleanly as a standalone predictive biomarker in independent prospective cohorts.

caveats: Small discovery cohort; response definitions not harmonized across later validation
IPRES overlaps heavily with TGF-β/EMT/stromal signatures and may not add orthogonal info beyond CD8 or TGF-β signatures
BRCA2 association not consistently replicated
Bulk transcriptomics conflates tumor and stromal signals
surprising_why: IPRES catalyzed the EMT/wound-healing axis of ICI resistance concept, but subsequent validation has been mixed: as a predictive biomarker it has underperformed relative to CD8 TIL, PD-L1, TMB, and broader TGF-β/fibroblast signatures. A landmark hypothesis refined rather than confirmed.

key_finding: In KRAS-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, co-mutations in STK11/LKB1 define a 'KL' subgroup exhibiting primary resistance to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade. KL tumors had markedly lower response and shorter PFS/OS despite comparable TMB, and were enriched for PD-L1-negative, non-T-cell-inflamed microenvironment; Stk11 loss in syngeneic Kras-mutant mouse LUAC was causally sufficient.
intervention: PD-1/PD-L1 blockade (nivo, pembro, atezo) in KRAS-mutant NSCLC stratified by STK11 and TP53; mouse Kras/Stk11 LOF
outcome: KL ORR 7.4% (SU2C) and 0% (CheckMate-057) vs 35.7%/57.1% for KP; STK11/LKB1 strongest negative correlate of PD-L1 positivity in TMB-intermediate/high LUAC
effect_size: ORR 7.4% KL vs 35.7% KP (P<0.001); PFS and OS significantly shorter in KL

confidence.reasoning: Multi-cohort retrospective analysis (>900 LUAC genomics; 174 ICI outcomes) plus CheckMate-057 validation and causal mouse genetics. KL resistance phenotype independently replicated in subsequent NSCLC ICI datasets.

caveats: Retrospective ICI cohorts with heterogeneous regimens
CheckMate-057 KL subgroup extremely small (n=6)
STK11 alterations co-occur with KEAP1; disentangling contributions required later work
surprising_why: Established that a tumor-suppressor comutation — not TMB or PD-L1 alone — can be the dominant determinant of primary ICI resistance within a TMB-high histology, decoupling TMB from ICI response and reshaping NSCLC biomarker thinking.

key_finding: In KEYNOTE-024 (305 patients, advanced NSCLC, PD-L1 TPS ≥50%, EGFR/ALK-wildtype), first-line pembrolizumab monotherapy significantly improved PFS and OS versus platinum-doublet chemotherapy with fewer grade 3-5 AEs — establishing PD-L1 high expression as a predictive biomarker enabling single-agent ICI as 1L standard of care in NSCLC.
intervention: Pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W vs investigator-choice platinum-doublet, 1L, PD-L1 TPS ≥50%
outcome: Superior PFS and OS with pembro; higher ORR, longer DoR, lower toxicity

confidence.reasoning: Randomized phase 3, positive primary/secondary endpoints, FDA/EMA approval basis. 5-year follow-up (Reck 2021) confirmed durable OS benefit despite crossover.

caveats: Restricted to PD-L1 TPS ≥50%, EGFR/ALK-wildtype — not generalizable to PD-L1-low or driver-mutant NSCLC
Crossover (~66%) diluted OS benefit
Chemo control was investigator's choice, not a single regimen

key_finding: Phase 3 trial of 676 previously treated HLA-A*0201+ metastatic melanoma patients: ipilimumab (3 mg/kg) ± gp100 peptide vaccine significantly improved OS vs gp100 alone — the first therapy ever to demonstrate OS benefit in metastatic melanoma and the registrational study for the first approved immune checkpoint inhibitor.
intervention: Ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W × 4 ± gp100 peptide vaccine vs gp100 alone
outcome: mOS ~10 mo in ipi arms vs 6.4 mo with gp100 alone; durable long-term survival plateau in ~20% of ipi-treated patients
effect_size: Ipi+gp100 vs gp100: mOS 10.0 vs 6.4 mo (HR 0.68, P<0.001); ipi alone vs gp100: 10.1 vs 6.4 mo (HR 0.66, P=0.003)

confidence.reasoning: Randomized phase 3 with OS primary endpoint, confirmed by Robert 2011 (ipi + dacarbazine). Long-term pooled analyses show durable 20-26% OS plateau extending to 10 years, definitively establishing checkpoint blockade.

caveats: Restricted to HLA-A*0201+ patients
Control arm (gp100) now considered inadequate
3 mg/kg dose later shown inferior to 10 mg/kg for OS
irAEs novel, sometimes severe; 2.1% treatment-related deaths
surprising_why: Prior to this trial, no therapy had ever prolonged OS in metastatic melanoma despite decades of chemotherapy, IL-2, and vaccines. The durable survival plateau — a subset of patients effectively cured by releasing an endogenous T-cell response — was transformative and inaugurated the modern era of cancer immunotherapy.

key_finding: First large phase 1 trial of anti-PD-1 (BMS-936558, later nivolumab) in 296 patients with advanced solid tumors: durable objective responses in melanoma (28%), NSCLC (18%), and RCC (27%) with manageable irAEs. Pretreatment tumor PD-L1 IHC correlated with response (9/25 PD-L1+ vs 0/17 PD-L1− patients), providing the first clinical evidence that PD-L1 could serve as a predictive biomarker.
intervention: Nivolumab 0.1-10 mg/kg Q2W, phase 1 dose-escalation/expansion
outcome: Durable objective responses (≥1 year in 20/31 evaluable responders); immune-related AEs including pneumonitis (3 fatal cases)
effect_size: ORR 28% melanoma (26/94), 18% NSCLC (14/76), 27% RCC (9/33); grade 3/4 drug-related AEs 14%; PD-L1+ vs PD-L1− ORR 36% vs 0% (P=0.006)

confidence.reasoning: Large phase 1 with central pathology; results rapidly confirmed by CheckMate-017/-057/-066/-025 → broad regulatory approvals.

caveats: Single-arm phase 1
PD-L1 IHC assay (5H1, ≥5% threshold) differs from later companion diagnostics
Small PD-L1 biomarker subset (n=42)
Pneumonitis deaths highlighted novel, potentially fatal toxicity class
surprising_why: Durable responses in NSCLC — historically considered non-immunogenic — were contrary to prevailing assumptions and, with the melanoma data, showed PD-1 blockade has broad activity across histologies. Directly motivated the explosion of PD-1/PD-L1 trials across essentially every solid tumor.

key_finding: In metastatic urothelial carcinoma (IMvigor210) on atezolizumab, lack of response associated with a TGF-β signaling signature in peritumoral fibroblasts (F-TBRS) and a CD8+ T-cell-excluded phenotype in which T cells are trapped in collagen-rich stroma. In the syngeneic EMT6 model, combined anti-TGFβ (1D11) + anti-PD-L1 reduced stromal TGFβ, permitted T-cell penetration, and drove regression.
intervention: Atezolizumab monotherapy in IMvigor210; anti-TGFβ (1D11) + anti-PD-L1 in EMT6 mice
outcome: F-TBRS predicted non-response; dual TGFβ/PD-L1 blockade converted excluded to inflamed tumors in mice
effect_size: ~298 patients with evaluable transcriptomes; non-responders elevated in pan-fibroblast TGFβ response signature; EMT6 mouse combination produced complete/near-complete regression

confidence.reasoning: Large clinical cohort, multi-modal data, independent mouse validation. TGFβ/stromal signatures replicated across subsequent datasets.

caveats: Patient correlation is associative; causal TGFβ role established in one mouse model
F-TBRS overlaps with broader stromal/EMT programs
Subsequent clinical trials of TGFβ-targeted agents (bintrafusp alfa) have largely failed to confirm therapeutic promise
surprising_why: Despite compelling biology, clinical programs targeting TGFβ (bintrafusp alfa TGFβRII-trap/anti-PD-L1 bifunctional) failed pivotal trials (INTR@PID lung 037 discontinued 2021; biliary tract trial halted) — a translation gap where a biologically robust target has not yielded clinical benefit, possibly reflecting redundancy, cardiotoxicity dose-limiting, or context-specific TGFβ effects.

key_finding: Quadruple-mutant (Apc/Kras/Tgfbr2/Trp53) intestinal-stem-cell-driven mice develop MSS metastatic colorectal tumors with T-cell exclusion and TGFβ-activated stroma refractory to anti-PD-1/PD-L1 alone; pharmacologic TGFβ inhibition (galunisertib) unleashes Th1/CTL response that prevents metastasis; sequencing TGFβ blockade before or with anti-PD-L1 renders established liver metastases susceptible.
intervention: Galunisertib (TGFβR1 inhibitor) ± anti-PD-L1/anti-PD-1 in GEMM of metastatic MSS CRC
outcome: TGFβ blockade converted immune-excluded to T-cell-infiltrated Th1-polarized tumors; combination eradicated established liver metastases
effect_size: Dramatic regression of established liver mets with combination vs no benefit from anti-PD-1/PD-L1 alone; long-term survival

confidence.reasoning: Genetically defined GEMM recapitulating MSS CRC biology, orthogonal pharmacologic + genetic perturbations, immune depletion showing CD8 dependence; co-published with Mariathasan 2018.

caveats: Single GEMM; human MSS CRC is heterogeneous
Galunisertib oncology development discontinued
Mouse model has Tgfbr2 loss in epithelium while TGFβ is inhibited systemically; may not mirror human CRC where stromal TGFβ is dominant
surprising_why: Dramatic preclinical efficacy in MSS CRC (notoriously ICI-cold) generated major clinical enthusiasm, yet subsequent trials of TGFβ-pathway inhibitors + PD-1 in MSS CRC (bintrafusp alfa, NIS793 combinations, galunisertib + durvalumab) have produced only modest or negative results — a reproducibility gap between GEMM and human MSS CRC.

key_finding: In metastatic melanoma patients on pembrolizumab, pre-existing CD8+ T cells at the invasive tumor margin and inside the tumor, co-localized with PD-1/PD-L1 expression and a more clonal TCR repertoire, predicted response. On-treatment proliferation of intratumoral CD8+ T cells correlated with radiographic tumor shrinkage. PD-1 blockade works by reinvigorating a pre-existing adaptive immune response rather than de novo priming.
intervention: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) monotherapy in metastatic melanoma, pre- and on-treatment biopsies
outcome: Pre-treatment CD8 density at invasive margin predicted response; responders showed on-treatment intratumoral CD8 proliferation correlating with regression
effect_size: Baseline CD8 density at invasive margin AUC ~0.9 in discovery (n=46); validated independent cohort (n=15); TCR clonality higher in responders

confidence.reasoning: Quantitative IHC/IF on paired biopsies, independent validation, TCR sequencing support; the core finding (pre-existing CD8 predicts response) has been replicated across dozens of studies and is the basis of the 'inflamed/excluded/desert' framework.

caveats: Small single-institution cohorts (46 + 15 validation), melanoma only
IHC-based predictor not standardized; quantitative thresholds vary
Does not address responders with low baseline CD8 (~15-20%)
Pre-existing CD8 is necessary but not sufficient — many CD8-infiltrated tumors still fail

key_finding: scRNA-seq of 16,291 immune cells from 48 checkpoint-blockade-treated melanoma tumors identified two CD8+ T cell states: a memory/stem-like state marked by TCF7 associated with response/regression, and a dysfunctional/exhausted state (high inhibitory receptors, low TCF7) associated with progression. TCF7+/TCF7− CD8 ratio, and TCF7+ CD8 frequency by IF, predicted clinical outcome in an independent cohort.
intervention: Observational: anti-PD-1 and/or anti-CTLA-4 in melanoma (scRNA-seq + validation IF cohort)
outcome: TCF7+ CD8 frequency at baseline and on-treatment predicted response; epigenetic/clonal analysis supported stem-like origin
effect_size: TCF7+ CD8 frequency significantly associated with response in independent validation cohort

confidence.reasoning: Large scRNA-seq dataset, independent IF validation, concordance with Siddiqui 2019, Miller 2019, Im 2016. Framework broadly accepted.

caveats: scRNA-seq snapshots miss rare states, subject to dropout
TCF7+ CD8 in tumors may include tumor-reactive stem-like + bystander memory; tumor-reactivity not directly verified for all TCF7+ cells
TCF7-based response prediction not standardized as a clinical assay
Associative

key_finding: The HMG-box transcription factor TOX is both necessary and sufficient to program the CD8+ T cell exhaustion transcriptional and epigenetic state during chronic antigen stimulation (LCMV Cl13 and tumor models). TOX is induced via calcineurin/NFAT2 and then acts in a feed-forward, calcineurin-independent manner; TOX-deficient CD8+ cells fail to establish the exhausted chromatin landscape, undergo terminal differentiation or death, and cannot sustain long-term responses.
intervention: Conditional Tox knockout and overexpression in mouse CD8+ T cells (LCMV chronic; tumors)
outcome: Tox loss prevented canonical exhausted CD8+ formation; Tox overexpression in effectors induced exhausted-like program
effect_size: Near-complete loss of exhausted CD8+ in Tox-KO chronic infection; large-scale ATAC-seq chromatin accessibility changes

confidence.reasoning: Multi-lab concordance (co-published with Scott 2019, Seo 2019, Yao 2019), genetic LOF/GOF, transcriptomic + epigenomic validation, consistent findings in chronic infection and tumor models.

caveats: Tox ablation impairs both exhaustion and long-term persistence — TOX-KO cells die faster
Predominantly mouse; human TOX clinical targeting remains preclinical
Exhaustion is a survival adaptation; loss of TOX may remove a restraint on effector-to-death transitions rather than unleashing durable effector function
surprising_why: Early hope was that TOX could be therapeutically antagonized to reverse exhaustion; subsequent work showed TOX-deficient exhausted cells are not better effectors but fail to persist. TOX is more accurately a cell-intrinsic survival/differentiation program under chronic antigen than a simple brake on effector function — tempering therapeutic tractability.

key_finding: A Tcf1(TCF7)+PD-1+ CD8+ T cell subset within tumors has stem-like properties, co-expressing features of central memory and exhausted cells, and is the proliferative source that expands upon checkpoint blockade; it gives rise to terminally differentiated Tcf1−PD-1+ effectors. Ablation of Tcf1+PD-1+ TILs abolishes the therapeutic response to checkpoint blockade, demonstrating ICI efficacy depends on a stem-like progenitor pool rather than on direct reinvigoration of terminally exhausted cells.
intervention: Checkpoint blockade (anti-PD-1/PD-L1, ± vaccination) in mouse tumor models; genetic ablation and Tcf7 deletion
outcome: Tcf1+PD-1+ TILs proliferate and self-renew after ICI; Tcf1 loss abrogates stem-like function; depletion of Tcf1+ TILs eliminates ICI therapeutic benefit
effect_size: Complete abrogation of checkpoint blockade efficacy upon ablation of Tcf1+ subset; the subset accounts for the proliferative burst in responders

confidence.reasoning: Convergent with Sade-Feldman 2018, Miller 2019, Im 2016 Nature; genetic perturbation + adoptive transfer establish causal role; validated in human melanoma TILs.

caveats: Mouse tumor models (B16, MC38) may not fully capture diverse human TMEs
Human Tcf1+PD-1+ cells in blood/tumor are rarer and harder to quantify
Whether Tcf1+PD-1+ cells are broadly tumor-reactive vs. include bystander memory is debated
Upstream signals maintaining the Tcf1+ progenitor pool are not resolved
surprising_why: Contradicted the then-dominant view that PD-1 blockade 'reinvigorates' exhausted effector cells; instead efficacy requires a distinct memory-like progenitor population — reframing ICI mechanism and explaining why terminally exhausted tumors fail to respond even when heavily PD-1+.

key_finding: Chen and Mellman propose the 'cancer-immune set point' framework: an individual's likelihood of responding to immunotherapy is determined by the net balance of stimulatory factors (neoantigens, T-cell infiltration, MHC expression, immunogenic cell death) and inhibitory factors (checkpoints, Tregs, MDSCs, TGFβ, metabolic barriers, microbiome) integrated across the cancer-immunity cycle. Clinically, tumors can be classified as inflamed, immune-excluded, or immune-desert, each reflecting distinct failure points that suggest tailored combinations.

confidence.reasoning: Synthesis review by originators of the cancer-immunity cycle framework; inflamed/excluded/desert taxonomy broadly adopted.

caveats: Conceptual framework; predictions must be validated empirically per tumor type
Tripartite classification simplifies heterogeneity within each category
'Set point' metaphor can mislead if interpreted as fixed; it is dynamic

key_finding: Sharma, Hu-Lieskovan, Wargo, Ribas establish the canonical taxonomy of ICI resistance: primary (tumor never responds; low TMB, antigen-presentation defects, low PD-L1, no TIL, WNT/β-catenin, PTEN loss, MYC), adaptive (tumor adapts via IFN-γ-induced PD-L1, IDO, additional checkpoints), and acquired (relapse after response; B2M loss, JAK1/2 LOF, neoantigen loss). Provides the conceptual scaffold for resistance and combination strategies.

confidence.reasoning: Authoritative synthesis by leading investigators; primary/adaptive/acquired framework is standard nomenclature in trial design and regulatory thinking.

caveats: Review without new primary data
Boundaries between primary/adaptive/acquired not always sharp in individual patients
Some specific mechanisms cited are well-documented but rare
Predates Siddiqui/Sade-Feldman stem-like CD8 biology, TOX (2019), and microbiome work (Routy/Gopalakrishnan 2018)

key_finding: In 112 melanoma patients on anti-PD-1, responders had higher gut alpha diversity and enrichment of Ruminococcaceae/Faecalibacterium; non-responders were enriched for Bacteroidales. Fecal transfer from responder patients into germ-free mice enhanced antitumor immunity and anti-PD-1 efficacy — causal support for a microbiome-immunity link.
intervention: Anti-PD-1 (nivolumab or pembrolizumab); fecal microbiota transfer into germ-free mice
outcome: Higher alpha diversity and Ruminococcaceae abundance associated with response; responder-FMT mice showed improved tumor control and increased intratumoral CD8+
effect_size: Fecal shotgun cohort n=43 (30 R vs 13 NR); alpha diversity P<0.01; Faecalibacterium abundance P<0.01

confidence.reasoning: Well-powered single-institution (MDACC) cohort with 16S + shotgun metagenomics plus mechanistic germ-free mouse transfer; Science landmark.

caveats: Single-center (MDACC, Texas) — geographic/diet bias
Small fecal shotgun sub-cohort (n=43)
Associations may reflect diet/lifestyle confounders
Specific responder taxa have not consistently replicated across geographies
surprising_why: Specific responder-associated taxa (Faecalibacterium, Ruminococcaceae) have shown inconsistent replication across independent geographic cohorts. Meta-analyses (Lee 2022, McCulloch 2022 Nat Med) found no single taxon reliably predicts anti-PD-1 response, and the Gopalakrishnan signatures often disagree with parallel Routy (Akkermansia) and Matson (Bifidobacterium) studies — underscoring cohort-specificity of the microbiome-response signal despite a robust aggregate effect.

key_finding: In advanced NSCLC, RCC, and urothelial cancer on anti-PD-1/PD-L1, antibiotic exposure (within 2 months before or 1 month after ICI) shortened PFS and OS. Metagenomic stool profiling linked response to Akkermansia muciniphila; oral A. muciniphila restored anti-PD-1 efficacy after FMT from non-responders into mice via IL-12-dependent recruitment of CCR9+CXCR3+CD4+ T cells.
intervention: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 + antibiotic exposure (observational); FMT and oral A. muciniphila in mice
outcome: Antibiotics shortened OS/PFS; A. muciniphila abundance correlated with benefit; oral A. muciniphila rescued ICI efficacy in non-responder-FMT mice
effect_size: Antibiotic use associated with reduced OS (e.g., NSCLC mOS ~8 vs ~20 mo; HR ~3.5); discovery cohort ~249 patients

confidence.reasoning: Large multi-tumor cohort with metagenomic + mechanistic mouse validation; influential Science landmark.

caveats: Antibiotic analysis observational and confounded by indication
Akkermansia cutoffs/directionality not consistently replicated
Mouse MCA-205 and subcutaneous tumors may not recapitulate human epithelial cancers
FMT engraftment in mice differs from humans
surprising_why: Akkermansia muciniphila was initially presented as a clean positive biomarker, but follow-up (Derosa et al. 2022 Nat Med) showed a non-monotonic relationship — patients with very high relative abundance had worse outcomes than intermediate — complicating its use as a simple predictor. The antibiotic-harm signal has replicated more robustly than the specific Akkermansia taxonomy.

key_finding: Differences in spontaneous antitumor immunity between genetically identical C57BL/6 mice from Jackson vs Taconic vendors were eliminated by cohousing or FMT and traced to commensal Bifidobacterium. Oral Bifidobacterium alone improved B16 melanoma control comparably to anti-PD-L1, and the combination nearly abolished tumor growth, acting via enhanced DC function and CD8+ priming.
intervention: Oral Bifidobacterium supplementation ± anti-PD-L1
outcome: Reduced B16.SIY growth; increased tumor-specific CD8+ priming and infiltration; enhanced DC activation
effect_size: Bifidobacterium-alone effect comparable to anti-PD-L1 monotherapy; combination near-complete tumor control

confidence.reasoning: Rigorous gnotobiotic/vendor-swap design, cohousing controls, FMT causality; Science landmark. Clinical translation more mixed.

caveats: Single mouse tumor model (B16) in main experiments
Bifidobacterium operationally defined by 16S at genus level
Human studies have not consistently recapitulated a Bifidobacterium-response association
Effects may reflect broader community context
surprising_why: The striking preclinical effect of a single genus has not cleanly replicated in human anti-PD-1 cohorts — Matson 2018 reported Bifidobacterium longum as response-associated in melanoma, but Gopalakrishnan (2018) and Routy (2018) highlighted different taxa. Cross-cohort meta-analyses (Lee 2022, McCulloch 2022) have not confirmed Bifidobacterium as a robust human responder marker — the mouse vendor-effect mechanism does not translate simply to heterogeneous human microbiomes.

key_finding: Antitumor efficacy of CTLA-4 blockade in mice depended on gut Bacteroides species (B. thetaiotaomicron and B. fragilis): antibiotic-treated and germ-free mice failed to respond, rescued by gavage with B. fragilis, B. fragilis polysaccharide, or adoptive transfer of B. fragilis-specific T cells. FMT from ipilimumab-treated melanoma patients into mice recapitulated the association.
intervention: Anti-CTLA-4 + microbiota manipulation (antibiotics, germ-free, B. fragilis gavage, patient-FMT)
outcome: CTLA-4 blockade lost efficacy in microbiota-depleted mice; restored by Bacteroidales exposure; T-cell responses to B. fragilis/B. theta correlated with patient efficacy
effect_size: Qualitative full loss and restoration of anti-CTLA-4 efficacy across conditions

confidence.reasoning: Multiple converging gnotobiotic and immunologic manipulations demonstrating causality in mice, plus supportive patient T cell data; co-landmark with Sivan.

caveats: CTLA-4 mouse models may not fully recapitulate human ipilimumab biology
Patient T cell response cohort small and correlative
Identified 'beneficial' Bacteroides taxa do not consistently overlap with those identified in later anti-PD-1 human cohorts
surprising_why: B. fragilis was highlighted as a beneficial CTLA-4-cooperating microbe, yet in subsequent anti-PD-1 studies (including Gopalakrishnan 2018) Bacteroidales enrichment was often associated with non-response — a sign-flip depending on checkpoint target or cohort. This discordance across CTLA-4 vs PD-1 microbiome studies has not been fully reconciled, and B. fragilis as a universal 'good' microbe has not held up.

key_finding: Phase I trial: FMT from two anti-PD-1 long-term responder donors followed by anti-PD-1 reinduction in 10 patients with anti-PD-1-refractory metastatic melanoma produced objective responses in 3 patients (1 CR, 2 PR). Responders showed donor-like gut microbiota engraftment and favorable immune changes in gut lamina propria and TME (increased CD8+ infiltration, IFN-γ signature).
intervention: FMT (colonoscopic + oral capsules) from anti-PD-1 responders + anti-PD-1 reinduction
outcome: 3/10 objective clinical responses in previously PD-1-refractory patients; donor-specific engraftment; favorable TME remodeling
effect_size: Response rate 30% (3/10) in a refractory population with expected response rate near 0%

confidence.reasoning: First-in-human randomized-donor FMT trial in Science with integrated microbiome/immune/clinical readouts. Small sample but clinical signal striking; replicated by Davar 2021.

caveats: Very small sample (n=10) with two donors
Open-label, single-arm
Anti-PD-1 reinduction alone could explain some responses, though unlikely at this rate in refractory cohort
Mechanism not resolved
surprising_why: That FMT could rescue responses in patients classified as anti-PD-1 refractory was unexpected — refractory disease had been widely assumed to reflect tumor-intrinsic immune escape (B2M/JAK loss, antigen loss), not a reversible host-microbiome state. The observation that a gut-based intervention could flip phenotype in a minority of refractory patients, alongside Davar 2021, shifted microbiome work from 'biomarker' toward 'intervention'.

key_finding: Single-arm phase II trial: FMT from anti-PD-1 responder donors followed by pembrolizumab in 15 patients with anti-PD-1-refractory advanced melanoma yielded clinical benefit in 6/15 (including objective responses). Responders showed donor-like microbiota engraftment, increased CD8+ T-cell activation, decreased IL-8+ myeloid cells, and distinct proteomic/metabolomic signatures consistent with microbiome-driven TME reprogramming.
intervention: Responder-derived FMT (colonoscopic delivery) + pembrolizumab in anti-PD-1-refractory melanoma
outcome: Clinical benefit 6/15 (including objective regressions); rapid durable microbiota perturbation; increased intratumoral CD8+ activation, reduced IL-8+ myeloid
effect_size: Clinical benefit rate 40% (6/15); ORR ~20% (3/15) in anti-PD-1-refractory population

confidence.reasoning: Independent replication of Baruch 2021 with larger sample, multi-omic mechanistic characterization, consistent direction of effect; Science.

caveats: Single-arm, no randomized control
Small sample (n=15)
Heterogeneous 'refractory' definitions
Long-term durability and optimal donor selection open
surprising_why: Convergent independent replication across two centers (Tel Aviv/Sheba and Pittsburgh/NCI) using different donors and delivery protocols was itself striking, and the mechanistic signature (IL-8+ myeloid suppression reversal) provided a plausible TME link not obvious from prior correlative microbiome studies.

key_finding: CheckMate 067 (945 untreated metastatic melanoma patients): nivolumab+ipilimumab produced substantially longer PFS than ipilimumab monotherapy, and nivolumab monotherapy was superior to ipilimumab. The combination showed its largest relative PFS benefit over nivolumab monotherapy in PD-L1-negative tumors, establishing combination PD-1 + CTLA-4 blockade as a new standard of care in advanced melanoma.
intervention: Nivolumab monotherapy vs nivolumab+ipilimumab vs ipilimumab monotherapy
outcome: mPFS 11.5 mo (nivo+ipi) vs 6.9 mo (nivo) vs 2.9 mo (ipi); long subsequent follow-up showed durable OS benefit with combination
effect_size: PFS HR 0.42 (nivo+ipi vs ipi); HR 0.57 (nivo vs ipi); PD-L1-negative subgroup PFS 11.2 (combo) vs 5.3 mo (nivo)

confidence.reasoning: Large randomized phase 3 with 6.5+ year follow-up showing durable OS benefit of the combination; practice-changing.

caveats: Grade 3-4 TRAEs in 55% of combination arm — substantial toxicity
Not powered for direct nivo vs nivo+ipi comparison at registration
PD-L1 subgroup analyses underpowered for definitive biomarker conclusions
Generalizability to non-melanoma tumors varies

key_finding: KEYNOTE-177 phase 3 (307 treatment-naive patients, MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC): first-line pembrolizumab doubled median PFS versus chemotherapy (16.5 vs 8.2 months, HR 0.60) with markedly fewer grade 3+ AEs, establishing PD-1 blockade as standard first-line therapy for dMMR mCRC.
intervention: First-line pembrolizumab 200 mg q3w vs investigator's choice 5-FU-based chemotherapy ± bevacizumab or cetuximab (chemo-to-pembro crossover on progression)
outcome: mPFS 16.5 vs 8.2 mo; ORR 43.8% vs 33.1% with far more durable responses (83% vs 35% ongoing at 24 mo)

confidence.reasoning: Large randomized phase 3 with clear PFS benefit; practice-changing for dMMR mCRC. Final analysis (2022) OS HR ~0.74 — not statistically significant due to crossover, a notable nuance.

caveats: Substantial crossover confounded OS analysis
Early progressors on pembrolizumab (~29% PD as best response) had worse outcomes than chemo in the first 6 months — sparked discussion of upfront chemo+IO
Restricted to dMMR/MSI-H subset (~4-5% of mCRC)
Heterogeneous chemotherapy comparator
surprising_why: The initial biphasic PFS curve — pembrolizumab underperforming chemotherapy in the first ~6 months before crossing over — was an unexpected pattern reflecting a substantial primary-resistance subset within dMMR mCRC, tempering the assumption that dMMR alone is a near-universal ICI predictor. The later failure to reach statistically significant OS benefit (likely due to crossover) was also a subtle disappointment.
